name: Cannonville
short_description: BLM public land near Cannonville offers rockhounders access to quality agate and jasper specimens in scenic Utah badlands terrain. Free rockhounding with standard BLM personal-use regulations.
description:
Cannonville sits within Utah's vibrant Paria Rimrocks region, a geologically diverse landscape where Cretaceous and Tertiary formations create excellent rockhounding opportunities. The BLM land surrounding Cannonville is known for producing colorful agate and jasper specimens, along with chalcedony and quartz formations exposed in washes and hillside outcrops. This area attracts rockhounders seeking quality specimens without commercial pressure, as it remains less crowded than fee-dig sites while maintaining excellent geological potential.

The terrain combines badlands topography with scattered desert vegetation, offering both accessible collecting areas and more challenging terrain for those willing to explore further. Colorful agate nodules and banded jasper can be found weathering out of hillsides and concentrated in ancient stream beds. The region's geological history includes significant mineralization during the Laramide Orogeny, when hydrothermal fluids deposited silica-rich minerals throughout the area.

Rockhounders should expect warm desert conditions and plan for the seasonal climate. The surrounding landscape offers scenic views across the Paria Plateau, making this a rewarding location for both specimen collectors and casual explorers. Water and shade are limited, so preparation is essential.
history: This area sits within the Colorado Plateau where Laramide mountain-building activity (80-55 million years ago) created hydrothermal conditions that deposited silica-rich fluids through fractures in Cretaceous and Tertiary rock layers. These conditions formed the agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens found weathering from hillsides and stream deposits today. The Paria Rimrocks region has long been known to local rockhounders for its accessible BLM collecting opportunities.
tips: Search washes and stream channels where agate and jasper nodules naturally concentrate after erosion - focus on areas where water has carved through hillsides. Look for colorful banded jasper on exposed hillsides; specimens often show red, orange, yellow, and brown striping. Bring a small hammer or rock pick to break free nodules from matrix, and use a loupe to examine color banding and translucency in the field. Early morning collecting is most productive, as the low sun angle makes colorful specimens easier to spot among desert rocks.
terrain_notes: The terrain consists of exposed badlands with scattered sagebrush and juniper, ranging from gently sloping hillsides to moderate washes. Elevation around 5,200-5,400 feet. Footing is rocky with loose material and occasional sharp shale fragments. Walking distances from pullouts typically range from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on specific collecting areas chosen. Some areas have unstable banks and loose rock requiring careful footing.
best_season: October - April. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) offer the most comfortable temperatures for desert rockhounding, with daytime highs in the 60-75 degree range. Summer heat (May-September) can exceed 90 degrees with intense sun exposure and limited water sources. Winter (December-February) is generally mild at this elevation but occasional snow can make access difficult.
written_directions: From Panguitch (approximately 30 miles north), take Utah State Route 12 east toward Escalante. Continue for about 20 miles and turn south toward Henrieville or Cannonville. Follow local roads to BLM access points near Cannonville. Specific pullouts and collection areas can be identified along the road shoulders and established rockhounding locations. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for some back roads, though passenger vehicles can reach main collecting areas via paved and well-maintained dirt roads.
road_conditions: Paved access via UT-12; secondary roads are graded dirt but generally passable to passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some pullouts and collection areas may require high-clearance vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along road shoulders and established rockhounding areas near Cannonville. No developed parking lot; rockhounders use wide spots in the road and informal turnouts. Limited capacity at most locations.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose material or specimens that can be removed by hand tools. Do not damage rock formations or create new excavations. Pack out all trash. Leave the landscape as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
No damage to rock formations
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Panguitch, approximately 30 miles north, has gas stations, restaurants, and water. Smaller services may be available in Henrieville or Cannonville but are limited.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain with rocky footing. No wheelchair access to most collecting areas. Uneven ground and loose rocks require sturdy footwear. Hillside collecting requires scrambling over uneven terrain. Overall suitable for most ambulatory rockhounders but not for those with significant mobility limitations.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find rockhounding near Cannonville, Utah?
faq.answer: The Cannonville BLM area is known for quality agate and jasper specimens, along with chalcedony and quartz formations. Agate nodules are commonly found in washes and stream beds, while colorful banded jasper appears on exposed hillsides. Most specimens show red, orange, yellow, and brown coloring typical of the Colorado Plateau region.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound on BLM land near Cannonville?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal-use rockhounding on BLM land near Cannonville. You can collect for free under standard FLPMA regulations: maximum 25 lbs per day, personal use only, no commercial collection, and hand tools only.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Cannonville?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) offer the most comfortable temperatures between 60-75 degrees. Summer heat often exceeds 90 degrees with intense sun, while winter can bring occasional snow. Avoid peak summer months unless you have early morning shade access.

faq.question: How far is Cannonville rockhounding from Panguitch, and what kind of vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: Cannonville is approximately 30 miles southeast of Panguitch via UT-12. A passenger vehicle can reach the main BLM collecting areas through paved and maintained dirt roads, though high-clearance vehicles may be needed for some secondary access points.

faq.question: Is Cannonville rockhounding good for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes. The terrain near Cannonville is accessible for beginners with basic rockhounding skills. Kids ages 6 and up can participate in collecting loose agate and jasper specimens. The area requires no special equipment beyond hand tools, and finding quality specimens is relatively straightforward since colorful pieces are visible on the ground.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring when rockhounding near Cannonville?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer or rock pick, screen for separating fine material, sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, plenty of water (the desert has limited sources), sun protection, and a backpack to carry your 25-lb daily limit. A loupe or magnifying glass helps identify quality specimens in the field. Arrive early to maximize collecting time before heat peaks.

meta_title: Cannonville Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Cannonville, Utah for agate, jasper, and quartz. No permit required. 25 lb/day limit. Perfect for beginners and families.
